title: Psalms 52
subtitle: Why Do You Boast of Evil?

heb.paragraph: "Why do you boast of evil, you mighty man (gibbōr)?" (v.1). Addressed to Doeg the Edomite, who reported David to Saul and slaughtered the priests of Nob (1 Sam 22). The "mighty man" is sarcastic: Doeg’s might is used for treachery, not valour. His tongue is a sharpened razor (v.2). He loves evil more than good, falsehood more than truth (v.3).

notes.note: "Why do you boast of evil? Your tongue plots destruction; it is like a sharpened razor." The tongue as weapon — the same speech-as-violence theme from Psalms 5, 10, 12.
"You love evil rather than good. You love every harmful word." The preference for destruction: the boaster chooses evil deliberately.

sections.header: Verses 5–9 — “I Am Like an Olive Tree Flourishing”

heb.paragraph: "But I am like an olive tree (zayit) flourishing in the house of God; I trust in God’s unfailing love for ever and ever" (v.8). The contrast: the boaster is uprooted (v.5); David is planted and flourishing. The olive tree image recalls Psalm 1’s tree by water. Trust in ḥesed produces rootedness; trust in wealth produces ruin (v.7).

notes.note: "God will bring you down to everlasting ruin. The righteous will see and fear and laugh at you: ‘Here is the one who did not make God his stronghold but trusted in great wealth.’" The contrast between Doeg’s fate and David’s.
"I trust in God’s unfailing love for ever and ever. I will praise you forever." The olive tree: productive, enduring, beautiful. Rooted in God’s house.

ppl.name: Doeg the Edomite
ppl.role: Saul's chief herdsman who betrayed the priests of Nob (superscription; 1 Sam 22)
ppl.text: Doeg informed Saul that Ahimelech had aided David, then personally executed 85 priests when Saul's own soldiers refused (1 Sam 22:18-19). The psalm describes him as one who 'boasts of evil' and whose tongue is 'like a sharpened razor' practicing deceit (vv.1-2). David contrasts Doeg's trust in 'great wealth' and 'destructive tongue' with the righteous who trust in God's unfailing love (v.8). Doeg represents the dangerous informer within a corrupt regime — power without conscience.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Fugitive who contrasts the fate of the treacherous with the faithful (vv.6-9)
ppl.text: David declares himself 'like an olive tree flourishing in the house of God' (v.8) — a tree that takes decades to mature but produces for centuries. Against Doeg's short-term power grab, David trusts in God's chesed 'for ever and ever' (v.8). The botanical metaphor reveals David's time horizon: the wicked are uprooted (v.5), while the righteous are planted and fruitful. This olive tree image becomes a key Pauline metaphor in Romans 11.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWhy do you boast of evil, you mighty hero? Why do you boast all day long, you who are a disgrace in the eyes of God?ESVWhy do you boast of evil, O mighty man? The steadfast love of God endures all the day.NIVFor what you have done I will always praise you in the presence of your faithful people. And I will hope in your name, for your name is good.ESVI will thank you forever, because you have done it. I will wait for your name, for it is good, in the presence of the godly.

rec.text: ""Why do you boast of evil? Your tongue plots destruction; it is like a sharpened razor." The tongue as weapon — the same speech-as-violence theme from Psalms 5, 10, 12."

rows.label: The Tongue as Razor — 52:1–4
rows.text: "Why do you boast of evil?" Doeg: treachery disguised as service. Tongue plots destruction.

hebtext.note: "Why do you boast of evil, you mighty man (gibbōr)?" (v.1). Addressed to Doeg the Edomite, who reported David to Saul and slaughtered the priests of Nob (1 Sam 22). The "mighty man" is sarcastic: Doeg’s might is used for treachery, not valour. His tongue is a sharpened razor (v.2). He loves evil more than good, falsehood more than truth (v.3).

"But I am like an olive tree (zayit) flourishing in the house of God; I trust in God’s unfailing love for ever and ever" (v.8). The contrast: the boaster is uprooted (v.5); David is planted and flourishing. The olive tree image recalls Psalm 1’s tree by water. Trust in ḥesed produces rootedness; trust in wealth produces ruin (v.7).

themes.note: Justice peaks: the treacherous tongue judged. Faith: the olive tree rooted in ḥesed. Judgment: the boaster uprooted.
